physDesc.p: Two separate manuscripts (fols. 1–118, fols. 119–261) probably bound together for Sir Walter Cope (Watson, 'Walter Cope', A63, p. 282, and A145, p. 287).

supportDesc.extent: iv (parchment fragment, two paper endleaves, and a parchment endleaf) + 263 leaves

binding.p: Blind-tooled calf, with centrepiece, over pasteboards, s. xvi ex or xvii in, with evidence of ties.

history.acquisition: Presented by Sir Walter Cope in 1602. The Benefactors' Register, p. 47 (but not the printed version in SC, p. 83), makes it clear that Cope's MS. contained both Philip Presbyter ln Job and 'Petri Blesensis Opera'.

recordHist.source:
Part 1: preliminary description by Jessica Hendy-Hodgkinson (March 2026) as part of INSULAR: Insular Manuscripts in the Age of Charlemagne (ERC/UKRI: EP/Z53402X/1); the description will be revised in again in the light of the project’s findings. Part 2 and endleaf: description adapted (2026) by Matthew Holford from the following sources with limited additional description:
Summary Catalogue (1922)
Otto Pächt and J. J. G. Alexander, Illuminated Manuscripts in the Bodleian Library, Oxford , III (1973), nos. 6 [part 1: decoration], 950 [part 2: decoration, dating, provenance]
Summary Catalogue, vol. 2, part 1, p. 312

ref.title: MLGB3: Medieval Libraries of Great Britain
MLGB3: Medieval Libraries of Great Britain

listBibl.head: Printed descriptions:
listBibl.bibl: A. G. Watson, Catalogue of Dated and Datable Manuscripts c.435–1600 in Oxford Libraries (Oxford, 1984), no. 88 [part 1]

msItem.author: Philippus Presbyter
msItem.title: Commentary on Job
msItem.rubric: (fol. 2v) Incipit liber commentarii Philippi praesbiteri ad Nectarium episcopum in libris sancti Iob
msItem.incipit: Uir erat in terra Chur nomine Iob. Cui dubium quod terra illa
msItem.note: Fol. 118v, in a different, contemporary (?) hand: 'Tractio super beatum iob usque dum legitur pepigi foedus cum oculis meis. Et deest pene iiia pars libri. Et hanc tractacionem philippus ad nectarium scripsit
Bks. 1–2 only; bk. 2 begins at fol. 61v.
A section of text on fol. 9r has been recopied in a 12th-century hand in interlinear spaces.

listBibl.bibl: pr. Basel, 1527 (USTC 679446; VD16 P 2459), from a now lost Fulda manuscript, and Paris, 1545 (USTC 149316; repr. Operum ... Bedae, Basel, 1563, vol. IV, col. 602ff), from Paris, Bibliothèque de l'Arsenal, 315
Michael M. Gorman, 'The Manuscripts and Printed Editions of the Commentary on Job by Philippus', Revue Bénédictine, 116/2 (2006), pp. 193-232, listing the present manuscript at p. 203

supportDesc.support: Parchment (calf). All quires arranged so that hair side is outside. Hair mostly faces flesh within the gatherings except in quires 2, 3, 5, 11, 12, 13, and 15 which are variously arranged.
supportDesc.foliation: Modern foliation added in the upper outer corner of each folio (ink).
supportDesc.collation: 1 (7) (fols 1r–7v) (regularly arranged except that fol. 7 is a singleton secured by a vellum strip woven between holes in the folio and a stub, possibly from the front endleaf, no loss of text), 2 (8) (fols 8r–15v; 2 and 7, fols 9 and 14, are singletons), 3–4 (8) (fols 16r–31v), 5 (7) (fols 32r–38v (fol. 34 is a singleton, no loss of text), 6 (8) (fols 39r–46v), 7 (8) (fols 47r–54v) (3 and 6, fols. 49 and 52, are singletons), 8–10 (8) (fols 55r–78v), 11 (10) (2 and 9, fols. 80 and 87, are singletons) (fols 79v–88r), 12–14 (8) (fols 89r–112v), 15 (6) (ff. 113r–118v). First quire numbered with a Roman numeral (I) with a dot either side roughly centralised in the lower margin of the last folio. All other quires marked with a letter (b-o) centralised in the lower margin of the last folio. No quire mark at the end of the final gathering.

layoutDesc.layout:
1 column, ruled in drypoint for 28–31 lines. Ruled space 238–249 170–181
Pricked and ruled after folding several bifolia at a time, depending on thickness. Ruling is faint on many folios; re-ruled in places. Single bounding lines. Slits in both inner and outer margins guided horizontal ruling. Quires 12–15 pricked twice, first with an awl and then with a knife to accommodate more lines of text on each folio.

handDesc.handNote: Copied by a single hand in Insular minuscule script, with close palaeographical similarities with three documents issued by Æthelwulf, king of Wessex, dated 838, 839, and 847: London, British Library, Cotton MS Augustus II. 37 (face), Cotton MS Augustus II. 20 (dorse, Wilton confirmation), and Cotton Ch VIII. 36 (Sawyer nos. 1438, 298): see J. Crick, ‘The Case for a West Saxon Minuscule’, Anglo-Saxon England 26 (1997), pp. 63–79. Fol. 1r and the first two lines of fol. 1v written in a higher-grade set minuscule, which uses an oc-a and straight-backed d. This script was also used for the opening lines of each book of the Commentary (on fols 2v and 61v). The remainder of the manuscript (until fol. 118r) written in a more cursive Insular minuscule, characterised by the elongated top strokes of g (to the left) and t (to the right); a sometimes has an open bow and the bow of p is likewise open, ending in a small foot. The colophon on f. 118v was written by a different Insular minuscule hand.

decoDesc.summary: Fine initials. (Pächt and Alexander iii. 6, pl. I)
decoDesc.decoNote: Initials drawn in the Insular style in the ink of the text, surrounded by red dots and coloured with a palette of red, blue, and yellow. Use of diminuendo. The initial group at the beginning of the prologue (on fol. 1r) decorated with leaf motifs. The initials at the beginning of each book of the commentary (on fols 2v and 61v) decorated with interlace. Minor initials on fols 17r, 23r, 25r, 32v, 34v, 48v, 53v, 64r, 67r, 71r, 74r, 77r, 84v, 85r, 89v, 101r, and 107v.

history.provenance: For date and origin see above, under Hand(s).
St Augustine's Abbey, Canterbury: Di.III.Gª IIII Liber sancti augustini Cantuariensis, late 13th-early 14th century, fol. iv verso.

St Augustine's Abbey, Canterbury: BA1.137 in the late-fifteenth century catalogue (ed. Barker-Benfield, I, 419; = BA7.7 = BA8.7); "cum A." (added to title, fol. 1r); seen by Leland at the abbey (MLGB3).

Sir Walter Cope, MS. 63 in Thomas James's list of his collection (see above). Fol. 1r '63', 'presumably Cope's number and the fact that it is trimmed shows that it was put on before the book was bound' (Watson).

msItem.author: Peter of Blois
msItem.title: Epistolae
msItem.note: The bulk of fol. 239v was originally blank; it was used by the scribe for the end of the following item.

msItem.author: Ps.-Peter of Blois
msItem.title: De XII utilitatibus tribulationis
msItem.note: Ends at the end of fol. 261r with 'regnum celorum secundum', a few lines being left untranscribed. Fol. 261v is blank.

layoutDesc.layout: Frame-ruled: 2 cols. of c. 50-55 lines, c. 225 c. 145 (65 + 15 + 65)

handDesc.handNote: Mixed script: secretary with many anglicana forms.

decoDesc.decoNote: Penwork border, initials (blue initials flourished red). (Pächt and Alexander iii. 950).

history.provenance: Arms of Bury St. Edmunds abbey in the first initial (fol. 119r), and initials 'W. B.' (William Babington, abbot 1446–1453) at the top and bottom of fol. 119r, a later additions.
Sir Walter Cope, MS. 145 in Thomas James's list of his collection (see above)

msItem.author: Averroes
msItem.title: Commentarium magnum on Aristotle, Metaphysics
msItem.note: A strip of parchment containing the commentary on bk. 9, c. 15, and fragments of the preceding and following sections, as pr. Aristotelis ... Opera, VIII (Venice, 1560), 272-3

revisionDesc.change: Matthew Holford: incorporate new description of part 1 by Jessica Hendy-Hodgkinson; minor updates to part 2; update description of endleaf.
Revised to incorporate all information in Watson, Pächt and Alexander, and SC.
James Cummings Up-converted the markup using https://github.com/jamescummings/Bodleian-msDesc-ODD/blob/master/convertTolkien2Bodley.xsl
